info.title: Opedd API
info.description:
# Welcome to the Opedd API 👋

Opedd is the licensing infrastructure for the open web — think of it as the **Stripe of content licensing**. Publishers register their content, set prices, and get paid. AI companies, researchers, and platforms purchase rights programmatically, get a license key, and can prove compliance on-chain.

Every license issued through Opedd is:
- ✅ Recorded in our immutable ledger
- ⛓️ Registered on Base (Coinbase's L2) for on-chain proof
- 📧 Confirmed via a Handshake Email to the buyer

---

## Quick start — 3 steps

**Step 1 — Get your API key**

Log in to your Opedd dashboard → Settings → API. Your key looks like `op_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx`. Keep it secret — it has full access to your publisher account.

**Step 2 — Make your first request**

```bash
curl https://api.opedd.com/api?action=articles \
  -H "X-API-Key: op_your_key_here"
```

You'll get back a paginated list of your licensable articles with pricing.

**Step 3 — Issue your first license**

```bash
curl -X POST \
  "https://api.opedd.com/api?action=purchase" \
  -H "X-API-Key: op_your_key_here" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "article_id": "<your-article-uuid>",
    "license_type": "ai",
    "buyer_email": "buyer@example.com"
  }'
```

That's it. You'll get back an `OP-XXXX-XXXX` license key, the buyer gets an email, and the license is registered on-chain.

---

## Authentication

All publisher endpoints require your API key in the `X-API-Key` header.

```
X-API-Key: op_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
```

A few endpoints are **public** — no key needed: `verify`, `registry`, `lookup-article`, `certificate`, `invoice`, `license-discovery`. These are safe to call from the browser or an AI agent.

---

## Base URL

```
https://api.opedd.com
```

---

## Rate limits

All rate-limited endpoints return these headers so you can handle limits gracefully:

| Header | Description |
|---|---|
| `X-RateLimit-Limit` | Max requests allowed in the window |
| `X-RateLimit-Remaining` | Requests left in the current window |
| `X-RateLimit-Reset` | Unix timestamp when the window resets |
| `Retry-After` | Seconds to wait before retrying |

---

## Error format

All errors follow the same shape:

```json
{
  "success": false,
  "error": "A human-readable description of what went wrong"
}
```

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `400` | Bad request — missing or invalid parameter |
| `401` | Unauthorized — invalid or missing API key |
| `402` | Payment failed |
| `403` | Forbidden — licensing not enabled for this article |
| `404` | Not found |
| `409` | Conflict — e.g. transaction already used |
| `429` | Rate limit exceeded |
| `500` | Internal server error |

---

## License types

Opedd supports three license types. Choose the right one for your buyer:

| Type | Label | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| `human` | Human Republication | Journalists, researchers, editorial reuse |
| `ai` | AI Training | Training datasets, fine-tuning, model pretraining |
| `ai_inference` | AI Inference | RAG pipelines, retrieval-augmented generation, real-time lookups |

---

## On-chain proof

Every license is registered on the **OpeddRegistry** smart contract deployed on Base Sepolia:

```
0xf2cb1347de16c362eb67549dc1521fc8ab2f8d6d
```

Registration is non-blocking — your API call returns immediately, and the on-chain write happens in the background. You can track `blockchain_status` in the Registry (`pending → submitted → confirmed`).

---

Questions? Reach us at [opedd.com](https://opedd.com).

tags.name: Content
tags.description:
## 📄 Content

Discover and browse the articles registered under your publisher account. These endpoints let you list your catalog, get details on a specific article, or look up any article by its URL — useful for checking whether a piece of content has a license available before attempting a purchase.

Articles are registered automatically when a buyer uses the Opedd widget on your site, or you can import them in bulk from your sitemap via the dashboard.

tags.name: Licenses
tags.description:
## 🔑 Licenses

The core of the Opedd API. Use these endpoints to issue licenses programmatically — one at a time or in bulk — and to verify that a license key is valid.

Every issued license:
- Gets a unique `OP-XXXX-XXXX` key
- Triggers a **Handshake Email** to the buyer with the certificate
- Is registered on-chain on Base (non-blocking)
- Is logged in the immutable event ledger

Verification is **public** — anyone can verify a license key without an API key.

tags.name: Registry
tags.description:
## ⛓️ Registry of Proof

The public Registry of Proof is a tamper-evident, on-chain verified record of every license ever issued through Opedd. It's fully public — no API key needed.

You can filter by license key, article, or publisher. Think of it as a public ledger — an AI company can point to a Registry entry as proof they have the rights to use a piece of content.

tags.name: Agent
tags.description:
## 🤖 Agent Endpoints

Built specifically for AI agents and automated pipelines. No pre-authorization, no redirects, no browser required.

The `agent-purchase` endpoint accepts a Stripe payment method ID or a USDC on-chain transaction hash and returns a license key immediately. The `license-discovery` endpoint provides a machine-readable `opedd.json` file that agents can fetch to understand what's available for licensing from any publisher.

tags.name: Publisher
tags.description:
## ⚙️ Publisher Tools

Utility endpoints for publishers. Check your API usage and revenue stats, generate AI crawler blocking policies, and embed the Opedd widget on your site.

ApiKeyAuth.name: X-API-Key
ApiKeyAuth.description:
Your publisher API key. Generate one in your Opedd dashboard under **Settings → API**. Keys are prefixed with `op_` and are 35 characters long.

> ⚠️ Keep your API key secret. It has full access to your publisher account. Never expose it in client-side code or public repositories.

title.example: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

LicenseType.description: `human` — republication rights for journalists and researchers. `ai` — training dataset use for model pretraining or fine-tuning. `ai_inference` — RAG and retrieval-augmented generation, where content is retrieved at inference time.

IntendedUse.description: How the licensee intends to use the content. Optional but recommended — it's recorded in the license and shown in the Handshake Email to the buyer.

example.error: Missing or invalid API key. Use X-API-Key header with your op_ key.

RateLimited.description: Rate limit exceeded — check `Retry-After` header for when to retry

example.error: Rate limit exceeded. Max 30 purchases per hour.

get.description:
Returns all licensable articles registered under your publisher account, ordered by creation date (newest first). Each article includes its current pricing for both human and AI licenses, plus lifetime sales stats.

This is the right starting point if you're building an integration that needs to browse your catalog — for example, to let a downstream buyer choose what to license, or to sync your article list to an external system.

**Pagination** — results are capped at 100 per page. Use `limit` and `offset` to page through large catalogs.

**Filtering by type** — if you pass `type=ai`, you'll only see articles that have an AI price set. Useful if you're building an AI-specific purchasing flow and don't want to show articles that aren't available for that license type.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Number of results per page. Max 100.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: Zero-based offset for pagination. To get page 2 with `limit=50`, pass `offset=50`.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: Filter to only articles that have a price set for this license type. Omit to return all articles regardless of pricing.

total.description: Total number of licensable articles (ignores pagination)

articles.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms
articles.description: How AI is transforming modern journalism

get.description:
Fetch full details for a single article by its UUID — including pricing, lifetime revenue, total licenses sold, and the 10 most recent licenses issued for it (anonymized to name/org).

This is useful for building a **license history view** or for verifying an article's current pricing before issuing a license programmatically.

> 💡 You can get an article's UUID from the `list articles` response, or from your Opedd dashboard by clicking into any article.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The UUID of the article. You can find this in the list articles response or in your dashboard.

200.description: Full article details with recent license activity

article.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms
article.description: How AI is transforming modern journalism

post.description:
Issues a single license for one article. This is the core of the Opedd API.

When you call this endpoint:
1. A unique `OP-XXXX-XXXX` license key is generated and recorded in the immutable ledger
2. The buyer receives a **Handshake Email** with their license certificate and a verification link
3. The license is registered on the Base blockchain (non-blocking — your response comes back immediately)
4. Your publisher webhook fires with a `license.issued` event (if configured)

This endpoint is intended for **publisher-initiated** workflows — for example, you've already collected payment via your own checkout and now want to issue a license. If you need the buyer to pay directly, use the Opedd checkout flow or the `agent-purchase` endpoint instead.

**Choosing a license type:**
- Use `human` for journalists, academics, or anyone republishing the article
- Use `ai` for companies adding content to a training dataset
- Use `ai_inference` for RAG pipelines and retrieval systems that query content at runtime

> ⚠️ The article must belong to your publisher account (matched by API key). Attempting to issue a license for another publisher's article will return a `404`.

**Rate limit:** 30 purchases per API key per hour.

article_id.description: UUID of the article to license. Must be owned by the API key's publisher account.

buyer_email.description: The buyer's email. They'll receive a Handshake Email with their license key, certificate PDF, and a link to verify on-chain.

buyer_name.description: Full name of the licensee. Included in the certificate and Handshake Email.

buyer_organization.description: The organization the license is issued to (e.g. `Acme AI Corp`). Shown in the certificate.

201.description: License issued — the buyer has been emailed their key

content.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

403.description: Licensing not enabled for this article

example.error: Licensing not enabled for this article

post.description:
Purchase licenses for up to **50 articles in a single request**. All items share the same buyer details and intended use. Perfect for AI companies that want to license a curated dataset in one shot.

Here's what happens under the hood:
1. All articles are validated upfront — if any are invalid, the entire request is rejected with a clear error message
2. Each license is issued, registered on-chain, and logged individually
3. A single consolidated email is sent to `buyer_email` with a table of all license keys
4. Your publisher webhook fires once per issued license

**Partial failures** — if individual items fail after the batch has been accepted (rare — e.g. a key generation collision), those items will have an `error` field instead of a `license_key` in the response. The `summary` object tells you exactly how many succeeded.

> 💡 Need more than 50? Split your items into multiple batch requests. The rate limit is 10 batch requests per hour, so you can process up to 500 articles per hour.

**Rate limit:** 10 batch requests per API key per hour.

items.description: Array of articles to license. Each item needs an `article_id` and a `license_type`.

buyer_email.description: Email to receive the batch license summary with all keys in a single email.

201.description: Batch processed — check `summary.failed` for any partial failures

licenses.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

get.description:
Verify that a license key is valid and retrieve its full details — including what article it covers, who it was issued to, and its on-chain proof. **This endpoint is public — no API key needed.**

This is the endpoint your compliance team, legal team, or automated pipeline should hit to confirm that a piece of content was properly licensed.

**What `valid: true` means:**
- The license key exists in the Opedd ledger
- The transaction has `status: completed`
- It has not been revoked

**Blockchain proof** — for every license, the response includes a `blockchain_proof` object showing whether it's been registered on Base and a link to the transaction on BaseScan.

**Machine-readable data** — for `ai` and `ai_inference` licenses, the response includes a `machine_readable` JSON-LD object. This is designed to be embedded in dataset cards, model cards, or compliance reports as structured proof of licensing.

```bash
# Anyone can verify — no API key needed
curl "https://api.opedd.com/api?action=verify&key=OP-A1B2-C3D4"
```

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The license key to verify, in `OP-XXXX-XXXX` format.

content.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

machine_readable.content_title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

get.description:
Returns a snapshot of your publisher account — total articles, active licenses, revenue, and how many events have fired in the last 24 hours.

Useful for building internal dashboards or monitoring integrations. You can poll this endpoint to track revenue in real time.

Check whether a specific article URL is registered in Opedd and has a license available — without knowing the article's UUID.

This is especially useful for browser extensions, AI agents crawling content, or any integration that encounters an article URL in the wild and wants to check if it can be licensed.

**Discovery headers** — in addition to the JSON body, the response includes HTTP headers for programmatic sniffing:

```
X-License-Available: true
X-License-Human-Price: 10
X-License-AI-Price: 50
X-License-Protocol: opedd/1.0
X-License-Purchase-URL: https://opedd.com/buy/c41371c2...
X-License-API: https://api.opedd.com/agent-purchase
```

An AI agent can do a `HEAD` request to any URL and check these headers before deciding whether to proceed with a purchase — no JSON parsing required.

parameters.name: source_url
parameters.description: The canonical URL of the article you want to check.

data.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

404.description: Article not found — either not registered in Opedd or licensing is disabled

post.description:
Purchase a license directly from an AI agent — no pre-authorization, no browser redirect, no human in the loop.

This endpoint is the backbone of Opedd's agentic licensing protocol. An AI agent can discover content via `lookup-article` or `license-discovery`, then call this endpoint with a payment method to get a license key in a single round-trip.

**Supported payment methods:**

**Stripe (`stripe_pm`)** — provide a `pm_...` payment method ID. The payment method must already exist in Stripe (e.g. saved to a customer). Opedd charges it, confirms the payment, and issues the license immediately.

**USDC (`usdc`)** — provide the hash of a completed USDC transfer on Base. Opedd reads the chain to verify the transfer amount and recipient before issuing. No double-spending: each `tx_hash` can only be used once.

**Finding the article** — you can provide either `article_id` (UUID) or `article_url` (canonical URL). The URL lookup normalizes trailing slashes and `www.` variants automatically.

> 🤖 **For MCP users**: if you're using the Opedd MCP server (`opedd-mcp`), this endpoint is called automatically by the `purchase_license` tool. You don't need to call it directly.

**Rate limit:** 10 purchases per `buyer_email` per hour.

article_id.description: UUID of the article. Either `article_id` or `article_url` is required.

article_url.description: Canonical URL of the article. Either `article_id` or `article_url` is required.

buyer_email.description: Email for the license certificate. For agents, this is typically the operator's email.

payment.description: Payment details. Choose `stripe_pm` for card payments or `usdc` for on-chain.

payment_method_id.description: A Stripe payment method ID (`pm_...`), already saved in Stripe.

tx_hash.description: Transaction hash of a completed USDC transfer to the Opedd platform address on Base.

chain.description: The chain the USDC transaction was submitted on. Use `base` for production.

201.description: License issued — key is ready to use

article.title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

402.description: Payment failed — the charge was declined or the USDC transfer couldn't be verified

example.error: Your card was declined. Please use a different payment method.

409.description: Conflict — USDC transaction hash already used

example.error: This USDC transaction has already been used to issue a license

get.description:
The Registry of Proof is Opedd's public, append-only record of every license ever issued. It's fully open — no API key, no account required. **Think of it as a public ledger for content rights.**

Every entry in the Registry includes the license key, article title, publisher name, licensee organization (if provided), the amount paid, and the on-chain transaction hash once confirmed.

**How to use it:**
- Query by `license_key` to look up a specific license
- Query by `article_id` to see all licenses for a particular article
- Query by `publisher_id` to see all licenses issued for a publisher's content
- Query with no parameters to get a live global feed of recent licenses

This endpoint is designed to power compliance dashboards, legal audit trails, and public transparency pages. If an AI company wants to publicly demonstrate they've licensed their training data, the Registry is how they do it.

parameters.name: license_key
parameters.description: Look up a specific license key.

parameters.name: article_id
parameters.description: Filter to all licenses for a specific article.

parameters.name: publisher_id
parameters.description: Filter to all licenses for a specific publisher's content.

records.article_title: The Future of AI in Newsrooms

get.description:
Returns a machine-readable `opedd.json` discovery file for a publisher — analogous to `robots.txt` or `sitemap.xml`, but for licensing.

AI agents and crawlers fetch this file to understand:
- How much content is available for licensing
- What the default prices are
- Which endpoints to use to look up and purchase specific articles
- Any special instructions the publisher has left for AI systems

**How to serve it on your own domain:**

Add this to your site's `<head>` or serve it at `/.well-known/opedd.json`:

```html
<link rel="opedd-discovery"
  href="https://api.opedd.com/license-discovery?publisher_id=YOUR_PUBLISHER_ID"
  type="application/json" />
```

Or redirect `yourdomain.com/.well-known/opedd.json` → this endpoint.

parameters.name: domain
parameters.description: Publisher domain — e.g. `publisher.com`. Opedd will look up the matching publisher.

example.agent_instructions: Use the lookup endpoint to check if a specific article is licensable. Then call agent-purchase with a Stripe payment method or a USDC transaction hash to get a license key immediately.

get.description:
Download a beautifully formatted PDF license certificate for any valid license key. **Public — no API key required.**

The certificate includes the license key, article title, publisher name, licensee details, license type, amount paid, and issuance date. It's designed to be attached to legal agreements or stored in a compliance archive.

```bash
# Download to a file
curl "https://api.opedd.com/certificate?key=OP-A1B2-C3D4" \
  --output license-certificate.pdf
```

Download a PDF invoice for a completed license purchase. **Public — no API key required.**

The invoice is formatted as a standard A4 document and includes:
- Bill From (Opedd / publisher) and Bill To (licensee)
- Issue date and Net-30 due date
- Archive coverage period
- A PAID or PENDING badge

Useful for buyers who need to submit a receipt to their finance team or include it in a procurement audit trail.

```bash
curl "https://api.opedd.com/invoice?key=OP-A1B2-C3D4" \
  --output invoice.pdf
```

parameters.name: publisher_id
parameters.description: Your publisher UUID. Find it in Settings → API in your dashboard.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: `robots` returns standard `robots.txt` format. `ai` returns `ai.txt` format for platforms that support it.

text/plain.example:
User-agent: GPTBot
Disallow: /

User-agent: ClaudeBot
Disallow: /

User-agent: PerplexityBot
Disallow: /

# This site's content is licensed via Opedd.
# To access content programmatically, visit: https://opedd.com

post.description:
Re-send all license keys associated with an email address in a single consolidated email. No authentication required — anyone can trigger a resend for their own email.

This is useful when a buyer has lost their license email or needs to forward it to a colleague. The email contains a table of all licenses associated with that address, with key, article title, type, and a verify link for each.

> 🔒 To prevent abuse, this endpoint is rate limited to **3 requests per email address per hour**. The response is always the same regardless of whether the email is found — this prevents email enumeration.

email.description: The email address to resend licenses to.

200.description: Request accepted — if licenses exist for this email, they've been sent
